Single-Molecule Imaging Reveals DNA Shape Read-out by the INO80 Chromatin Remodeler

Garp Linder,1 Daniela Horky,1 Sarah Zernia,1 and Johannes Stigler1, ∗

1 Gene Center, Ludwig-Maximilians-Universität München 81377 Munich, Germany

(Dated: November 27, 2025)

[Page 2]

Abstract Abstract: ATP-dependent chromatin remodeling enzymes act together to construct complex chromatin architectures around functionally important regulatory regions like promoters and origins of replication. The INO80 complex accurately positions nucleosomes bordering these regions, which is crucial for proper transcription start site selection and efficient replication. How INO80 localizes these nucleosomes and regulates its remodeling activity to produce specific positions mechanistically is unclear, although recent findings suggest a role for DNA shape features and barrier factors. Here, we use single-molecule DNA curtains to directly observe interactions of INO80 with DNA, nucleosomes and barrier factor Reb1. We show that INO80 recognizes DNA shape and strongly binds to shape features that are enriched in promoters and ORIs. Moreover, we find that INO80 performs 1D searches and intersegmental transfers to dynamically interact with nucleosomes and Reb1, but cannot bypass them, indicating that they could confine INO80 within promoters and thereby increase engagement with flanking nucleosomes. Our findings reveal how INO80’s target-site search and remodeling activity are influenced by DNA shape recognition and molecular architecture and elucidate how remodelers can integrate different kinds of information to transform the chromatin landscape. 6 Keywords: INO80; Nucleosome Remodeling; Single Molecule; DNA Curtains; DNA Shape

INTRODUCTION

Manipulation of nucleosomes, the core unit of chromatin, constitutes a major share of eukaryotic genome regulation and results in the formation of diverse chromatin states [1–3]. While histone 10 modifications are modulated by different epigenetic modulators, the positioning and histone com11 position of nucleosomes is mainly directed by four families of ATP-driven chromatin remodeling 12 complexes: SWI/SNF, ISWI, CHD, and INO80 [4]. Significant research efforts conducted in the 13 last two decades in S. cerevisiae have revealed that these protein families work in concert to 14 generate specific chromatin architectures around important regulatory regions like promoters and 15 origins of replication (ORI) [5–7]. The architectures produced are characterized by a nucleosome 16 depleted region (NDR) flanked by two well-positioned nucleosomes, which in context of promoters 17 were coined the +1 (over the transcription start site) and −1 (opposite side) nucleosome. Nucle18 osomes further up- and downstream show regular spacing and are phased relative to the +1/ − 1 19 nucleosomes [3, 8]. Maintenance of this architecture seems to be critical, as disruption has been 20 shown to promote cryptic transcription and reduce replication efficiency [7, 9]. The only chromatin 21 remodeler of the four families to accurately reproduce in-vivo-like +1/ − 1 nucleosome positions 22 in a in-vitro positioning assay was INO80 [10], a remodeler with nucleosome and hexasome sliding 23 activity and proposed functions in multiple other pathways, such as replication and DNA repair 24 [11–13]. INO80 is present almost exclusively on nucleosomes flanking NDRs [14] and its removal 25 enhances cryptic transcription [5, 9, 15], making it a prime candidate for genome wide positioning 26 of +1/ − 1 nucleosomes. Results from selective and simultaneous depletion of different remodelers 27 in vivo support this notion [5, 16]. In S. cerevisiae, INO80 is comprised of 15 subunits that can be grouped into three structural 29 modules, hereinafter referred to as the Core-, A-, and N-module [17] (fig. 1A). All three modules 30 are connected by the Ino80 subunit, which also contains the Snf2-like ATPase motor. During 31 repositioning of nucleosomes, INO80 forms an extended configuration, where the Core-module 32 binds to the nucleosome, while the A- and N-modules sit in front of the ATPase motor and interact 33 with the extranucleosomal DNA, which is translocated towards the motor [18, 19]. Using this 34 architecture INO80 positions nucleosomes by pulling them into the NDR [5].

The ability of INO80 to accurately slide nucleosomes to certain positions necessarily implies negative regulation of the sliding process through external cues. INO80 has been shown to position 37 and phase nucleosomes relative to other proteins bound to DNA, e.g., general regulatory factors 38 (GRF) Reb1 and Abf1 at promoters and ORC at ORIs, most likely through a ruler-like readout 39 [7, 20]. How this is facilitated mechanistically is unknown. Interestingly, INO80 is also able 40 to position +1/ − 1 nucleosomes in the absence of such ‘barrier proteins’, solely based on DNA 41 sequence inputs. Analysis of final +1 nucleosome positions remodeled in vitro by INO80 revealed 42 distinct DNA shape profiles, which changed upon mutation of different subunits [10]. Another study 43 observed that INO80 remodels nucleosomes with rigid linkers more slowly [21]. This suggests that 44 INO80 can regulate its remodeling activity through recognition of DNA shape features. DNA shape 45 sensing of proteins is mostly facilitated by interactions with the phosphate backbone of the DNA 46 and reads out characteristics of the DNAs three-dimensional structure like minor groove width, 47 propeller twist or DNA curvature [22]. Although structural and biochemical data has shown that 48 INO80 can recognize these structures [10, 19], it is not known how they influence INO80 binding 49 dynamics. Additionally, while the target-site search of transcription factors (TF) and other small DNA51 binding proteins (DBP) has been studied for decades [23–25], a comprehensive model for how 52 chromatin remodelers, including INO80, locate their target nucleosomes in a busy chromatin land53 scape is still missing. This is in part due to the limitations of classical biochemical bulk assays, 54 which are limited in their ability to observe interaction dynamics while bound to the substrate 55 and therefore cannot resolve molecular details of target site search. Recent single-molecule studies 56 of RSC, ISW2 and SWR1 in vitro have presented first insights by showing that these remodelers, 57 similar to most DNA-binding proteins, use 1D diffusion to locate target proteins like nucleosomes 58 on DNA [26, 27]. Similar in-vitro studies are still missing for INO80. In this study, we used single-molecule DNA curtains to address INO80’s target site search, 60 DNA binding preferences and interaction kinetics with Reb1 and H2A.Z-containing nucleosomes. 61 Additionally, we performed sliding assays to reveal the effect of DNA shape recognition on nu62 cleosome remodeling. We demonstrate that INO80 uses 1D diffusion by helical sliding to move 63 along DNA and can jump between DNAs using intersegmental transfers. We observe highly DNA 64 shape dependent binding and diffusion kinetics with stronger binding and slower diffusion speeds 65 on DNA sequences with high propeller twist angles, which are frequent in promoter NDRs [28]. 66 Using truncation mutants we identify the A-module as a sensor for these sequences and show that 67 their recognition within nucleosomal linker DNA leads to new intermediates during remodeling. 68 Additionally, we present dynamic interactions of INO80 with nucleosomes and Reb1 and find that 69 both act as barriers to INO80 diffusion, indicating that they could trap INO80 in NDRs and in70 crease engagement with the flanking +1/ − 1 nucleosomes. Together, this study reveals detailed 71 insights into how DNA shape, molecular composition and interaction partners govern INO80’s ki72 netics on DNA, laying the foundation for a comprehensive target site search model and providing 73 a framework for DNA shape dependent nucleosome positioning.

RESULTS INO80 diffuses along DNA

We expressed and purified the S. cerevisiae multi-subunit INO80 complex endogenously and labeled it fluorescently at the C-terminal ybbR-tag of the Ino80-subunit (fig. 1A). To investigate 78 INO80’s DNA binding and translocation, we used single-molecule DNA curtains, which allow the 79 visualization of individual INO80 complexes on DNA in real time and with high throughput. We

[Page 4]

used flow cells with nano-fabricated chrome barriers that were passivated with a lipid-bilayer, and assembled a double-tethered DNA curtain using end-modified λ-DNA molecules (48.5 kbp), 82 as previously described [29] (fig. 1A). A microfluidic system in combination with total internal 83 reflection (TIRF) microscopy allowed us to apply fluorescently labeled INO80 complexes to the 84 flow cell and record association and dissociation events of individual molecules. INO80 bound stably to λ-DNA with a mean lifetime of 113 ± 9 s and slowly moved along the 86 DNA in a non-directed fashion resembling a random walk (fig. 1B,C). As a control, we applied 87 a catalytically dead Cas9 mutant (dCas9) to the DNA, which, as expected, bound to its target 88 sites and showed no movement along the DNA (fig. 1B,C, fig. S1A). The analysis of single-molecule 89 diffusion coefficients revealed a wide distribution for INO80 with a median diffusion coefficient over −4 kbp2 /s for 90 30 times higher than for dCas9 (0.017 ± 0.001 kbp /s for INO80 vs. (5.5 ± 3.8) × 10 91 dCas9, fig. 1D). We hence conclude that INO80 translocates along DNA by 1D diffusion. A small subset of complexes diffusing on DNA showed noticeably higher fluorescence intensi93 ties, and photo bleaching analysis revealed that these complexes bleached in two steps (fig. S1B). 94 Analysis of all localized intensities displayed two distributions, indicating that at least ≈11 % 95 of DNA-bound INO80 complexes were dimers (fig. 1E). However, the diffusion coefficients and 96 lifetimes of dimeric complexes showed no significant differences compared to monomers (fig. S1C), 97 implying that these complexes are functionally indistinguishable from monomers in respect to their 98 DNA binding.

INO80 slides along DNA using a rotationally coupled mechanism

Having established that INO80 uses 1D diffusion, we sought to uncover how INO80 moves along 101 DNA mechanistically. DBPs have been shown to slide, hop or use a mix of both mechanisms [27, 30]. 102 This profoundly impacts the way the proteins interact with obstacles and interaction partners along 103 the DNA and is therefore crucial for understanding their target site search mechanism [31]. To 104 test whether INO80 hops or slides we compared INO80 diffusion at 120 mM KGlu with diffusion at 105 40 mM KGlu. As hopping proteins have to dissociate and re-associate continuously while moving,

[Page 5]

their diffusion coefficient is positively correlated to their off-rate and hence salt-dependent [30]. As expected, with reduced salt concentration, INO80’s median lifetime increased four-fold from 15.1 s 108 to 61.9 s. Its median diffusion coefficient decreased slightly, but the change was not significant 109 (0.012 ± 0.002 kbp /s to 0.017 ± 0.001 kbp /s; p = 0.055, Mann-Whitney U test, fig. 2A). This 110 implies that INO80 movement is mostly salt-independent and based on sliding.

Sliding proteins can diffuse by moving along the helical axis in a rotational fashion [32] or by 112 moving linearly over the DNA employing a rotationally decoupled mechanism [33]. To get insights 113 into INO80’s diffusion mode we analyzed the free-energy landscape along the protein’s path of dif114 fusion by calculating its roughness ε, which causes retardation of the effective diffusion speed due 115 to protein interactions with the DNA (fig. 2B). Using the median diffusion coefficient measured at 116 120 mM KGlu and a radius of ≈100 Å for INO80 (estimated from published cryo-EM structures), 117 we inferred a free-energy roughness of ≈1.75 kB T for rotationally coupled diffusion, while linear dif118 fusion would predict a much higher roughness of ≈3.15 kB T . Consistent with rotational movement 119 but incompatible with linear movement, models predict roughness values between 0.5 to 2 kB T 120 for efficient target site search [34], which has been confirmed for a wide variety of DBPs [32, 35]. 121 Additionally, the value for rotationally coupled movement closely fits the free-energy roughness 122 determined for Nhp6, a HMGB-type protein closely related to the DNA binding subunit Nhp10 123 within the INO80 N-module [36] (fig. 2C). Nhp6 bears a similar HMGB-type box domain as Nhp10, 124 has been shown to slide rotationally and induces high bending angles into the DNA during binding 125 [35]. Hence, we conclude that also INO80 moves by rotationally-coupled sliding along DNA and 126 possibly bends the DNA in the process (fig. 2D).

INO80 transfers between DNA strands

On the DNA curtains chip, DNA strands are juxtaposed, but tethering is not always perfectly parallel and multiple DNAs can tether within the same well (fig. S2A). This, and naturally oc130 curring thermal fluctuations allow the λ-DNA molecules to come into close contact and enables 131 proteins, if able, to jump between DNA strands (jumps in trans). We frequently observed these 132 jumps by INO80, which were characterized by an immediate change in x-position (fig. S2B). We 133 used a Hidden-Markov-Model (HMM) to estimate jump timepoints and compared displacements 134 around these timepoints with random displacements of INO80 complexes diffusing along DNA 135 (fig. S2C,D). Unlike random displacements, which were normally distributed, x-displacements at 136 jump timepoints showed a bimodal distribution. Intriguingly, the corresponding y-displacements 137 showed no significant difference compared to random y-displacements. This indicates that for these 138 kinds of jumps the complex does not undergo complete dissociation, 3D diffusion and re-association 139 during the transfer, but moves directly from one to the other DNA strand during random DNA140 DNA collisions, thus mechanistically fulfilling the definition for inter-segmental transfers.

Moreover, we also observed jumps between distant positions on the same DNA strand (cis), visible by changes exclusively in y-position (fig. S2E). The DNA is stretched to ≈74 % of its contour 143 length along the y-direction. Reptations and looping are hence highly limited in this direction and 144 far distant regions of the same DNA strand cannot come into close proximity. To perform cis 145 jumps, INO80 must therefore dissociate, diffuse in 3D space and re-associate at a different position 146 on the same DNA. These cis jumps should not be confused with the aforementioned systematic 147 hopping diffusion mechanism, but rather represent transitions from 1D to 3D to 1D, where the 148 protein by chance reassociates with the same DNA and can therefore cover much larger distances.

INO80 accumulates on DNA with high negative propeller twist

During closer analysis of INO80 trajectories, we noticed that diffusing INO80 complexes showed 151 varying mobility on λ-DNA over time. This non-random movement was represented by transitions 152 between highly diffusive and highly constrained segments within 1D trajectories of single complexes 153 (fig. 2E), an unexplained behavior also observed for RSC and ISW2 [27]. Given that INO80 154 is hypothesized to recognize DNA shape features and that our setup allowed us to analyze the 155 DNA sequence background of diffusing complexes, we tested whether INO80 binding dynamics are

[Page 7]

influenced by DNA shape properties. We chose propeller twist as it was singled out as one of the most relevant DNA shape parameter to explain nucleosome placement by INO80 in a previous study 158 [10]. The propeller twist Ψ measures the negative angle between the two bases of a base-pair and is 159 correlated with the electrostatic potential (EP) of the minor groove, which is emerging as a major 160 determinant of DNA shape read-out [37] (fig. S2F). We sorted diffusion coefficients of individual 161 complexes into quintiles depending on the average propeller twist that they had encountered along 162 their trajectory and compared them (fig. 2F). The analysis revealed a clear trend towards faster 163 diffusion at low Ψ with the lowest and highest quintiles showing a significant difference. Next, 164 we determined the localization probability density, the initial binding position and the off-rate of 165 INO80 complexes dependent on the DNA position and correlated it with the respective Ψ at this 166 position (fig. 2G). Strikingly, INO80 was strongly enriched on DNA sequences with high Ψ, showed 167 more initial binding to and longer lifetimes in these DNA regions. Given the strong correlations 168 we wondered whether other DNA shape features could explain INO80-DNA kinetics even better. 169 We tested 11 additional established intra-base pair and inter-base pair features, as well as two 170 minor groove parameters predicted by Deep DNAshape [38]. No features outperformed propeller 171 twist, but EP, stretch, opening, slide and rise performed equally well (fig. S2G). Together, this 172 supports INO80’s ability to directly recognize DNA shape features, and, additionally, that its 173 binding kinetics are dominated by these features. INO80 also showed lower off-rates in a segment of the λ-DNA between 12 to 18 kbp, which 175 exhibits a comparably low Ψ but contains multiple binding motifs found for INO80-subunit Nhp10 176 [39] (fig. 2G, red shaded area). This suggests that the N-module might additionally weakly recog177 nize sequence motifs during 1D diffusion, which may be relevant for different cellular functions of 178 the complex besides remodeling.
Removing INO80’s N-Module speeds up kinetics but retains DNA shape dependence

Having revealed that INO80 displays highly DNA shape dependent binding kinetics, we wondered how DNA shape recognition by INO80 is achieved. Given that the N-module is highly species 182 dependent and that its removal keeps INO80’s ability to reposition nulceosomes largely unaffected 183 [10, 19], we hypothesized that a mutant, missing the N-module and the N-terminal end of the Ino80 184 subunit would still retain its DNA shape readout capabilities. We chose an established ∆N-INO80 185 mutant from C. thermophilum that has been shown to be highly conserved in structure to S. cere186 visiae INO80 and which produces similar +1 nucleosome positions [10, 19] and applied it to DNA 187 curtains. This mutant will be referred to from here on as Ct. ∆N-INO80. If no species identifier 188 is given, the respective variant mentioned is a S. cerevisiae protein or mutant. Similar to the Sc. wildtype, Ct. ∆N-INO80 readily bound to λ-DNA and moved along the DNA 190 (fig. 3A), but displayed much faster diffusion dynamics with a median diffusion coefficient over 10 191 times higher than the Sc. wildtype complex (0.200±0.009 kbp /s vs. 0.017±0.001 kbp /s; fig. 3B). 192 Compared to the Sc. wildtype the calculated free-energy roughness for Ct. ∆N-INO80 was halved 193 (0.86 ± 0.15 kB T vs. 1.84 ± 0.07 kB T ) and consistent with values for DBPs without strong DNA 194 bending (fig. S3A). This validates our earlier observations that diffusion speed in the wild type 195 complex is mainly dominated by the high free-energy roughness of Nhp10’s non-sequence-specific 196 HMG-boxes. Remarkably, the diffusion speed of Ct. ∆N-INO80 complexes also showed a stronger 197 correlation with the mean propeller twist angles of the encountered DNA than the Sc. wildtype 198 complex (fig. S3B). Analysis of single trajectories revealed that steep transitions from high to low 199 Ψ on the DNA acted as strong barriers for Ct. ∆N-INO80, which ‘trapped’ the protein in DNA 200 regions with high Ψ (fig. 3C). Similar to the Sc. wildtype complex, other binding parameters of 201 Ct. ∆N-INO80 also correlated strongly with Ψ (fig. S3C), indicating that recognition of DNA

[Page 8]

shape is independent of the Ct. N-module and most likely an evolutionary conserved property of the combined Core- and A-module. As expected, the subset of long-living complexes near Nhp10 204 binding motifs also disappeared (fig. S3C). We again observed transfers between DNA strands without a change in y-position similar to 206 those of the Sc. wildtype, suggesting that the Ct. ∆N-INO80 complex architecture still enables 207 intersegmental transfers (fig. S3D).

The A-module is a sensor for DNA shape

After establishing that the N-module was redundant for DNA shape sensing, we turned our attention to the conserved A-module. The position of the A-module directly in front of the ATPase 211 motor and their shared connection via the Ino80 HSA/post-HSA domain suggest that it might play 212 a crucial role in recognizing DNA shape and regulating nucleosome sliding activity allosterically 213 [19]. We therefore purified and labeled the S. cerevisiae A-module, using a truncated version of 214 Ino80 (440–598), which binds all relevant subunits and forms a structurally fully cohesive A-module 215 [19]. A-module complexes on DNA curtains bound strongly to λ-DNA and showed very diverse kinet217 ics (fig. 3D). The majority of the complexes were almost static (S), while some complexes diffused 218 very rapidly (F). Surprisingly, on average, A-module complexes diffused slightly slower than the Sc. 219 wildtype complex and much slower than Ct. ∆N-INO80 (fig. 3B) with a median diffusion coefficient −3 kbp2 /s. Nevertheless, analysis of apparent diffusion speeds at specific positions 220 of (9.7 ± 0.8) × 10 221 revealed that kinetics were again dependent on DNA shape, with slow diffusion at positions with 222 high Ψ (fig. 3E, bottom; fig. S3E). Accordingly, also probability density, initial binding positions 223 and off-rates were highly dependent on the propeller twist of the DNA (fig. 3E, top; fig. S3F). The 224 fact that diffusion kinetics were very heterogeneous, highly DNA-shape dependent, but on average 225 almost static compared to Ct. ∆N-INO80, suggests multiple DNA binding configurations, which 226 in the full complex are additionally modulated by the associated Core-module. In line with this, 227 structures of the Ct. A-module show, that it can bind to DNA in at least two different states, with 228 or without additional protein-DNA contacts at the N-terminal end of the module [19, 40]. Taken 229 together, our data confirms the A-module as a DNA shape sensor that confers strong binding 230 preferences for DNA with high Ψ to the INO80 complex.

Influence of DNA-shape recognition on INO80 remodeling

With a clear picture of shape-dependent DNA kinetics by INO80 emerging, we wondered 233 whether DNA shape recognition by the A-module might also influence remodeling kinetics. In 234 gel-based sliding assays using nucleosomes with sufficiently long linker DNA, INO80 translocates 235 end-positioned nucleosomes into the center of the DNA [41]. A previous study had shown reduced 236 remodeling speed in gel-based sliding assays when native DNA sequences with high Ψ were in237 corporated into linker DNA [21]. However, the random distribution of high Ψ motifs across the 238 native linker sequences made it difficult to determine which interaction is responsible for this effect. 239 During a complete reaction cycle, the DNA is pushed 40 bp along different protein-DNA binding 240 interfaces, including the ATPase motor. Another study showed that sliding is strongly reduced 241 if a high Ψ motif is placed at SHL −6/7, where the ATPase motor contacts the DNA [19]. We 242 therefore designed nucleosome constructs with 80 bp linker DNA (0N80), whose average Ψ is uni243 formly distributed across the whole linker sequence and inserted a 10 bp long poly(dA:dT)-tract, 244 which due to its unique structure exhibits extremely high Ψ (fig. 4A). We chose SHL −12/13 as 245 the position for insertion, because only the A-module and N-module will contact it during the

remodeling process, according to current models and information from cryo-EM structures and footprinting [19, 40, 42, 43]. Improved separation of nucleosome species using gradient gels allowed 248 us to analyze the emerging remodeling intermediates. Remodeling of nucleosomes without poly(dA:dT)-tract produced four additional bands repre250 senting intermediates I1 –I3 and the fully remodeled 40N40 nucleosome R (fig. 4B), in line with 251 a translocation step size of ≈10 to 20 bp previously reported for INO80 [44, 45]. Strikingly, re252 modeling of the SHL −12/13 nucleosome produced an additional intermediate I4 close to the 253 fully remodeled product in all replicates, indicating an additional pause or termination point of 254 the sliding process between I3 and R. Furthermore, the fraction of fully remodeled product was 255 smaller for all timepoints, when the poly(dA:dT)-tract was present (fig. 4C). Reconstruction of 256 the approximate location of the poly(dA:dT)-tract within I4 (assuming ≈10 bp steps) places it 257 in direct contact with the A-module (fig. 4D). Our results therefore suggest that recognition of 258 poly(dA:dT)-tracts through the A-module can lead to abortion of the translocation process and 259 hence inhibition of remodeling.

INO80 dynamically interacts with nucleosomes

Next, we focused our attention on the role of interaction partners of INO80 within the context of target-site-search and remodeling. In vivo, INO80 preferentially interacts with and remodels +1 263 nucleosomes, which are enriched for the histone variant H2A.Z [46]. We therefore assembled labeled 264 H2A.Z-containing nucleosomes on λ-DNA and generated sparsely chromatinized DNA curtains to 265 which we added INO80 (fig. 5A).

[Page 10]

After injection, we observed INO80 co-localizing with nucleosomes over extended periods with a median interaction time of 15.3 ± 4.6 s (fig. 5B,C; fig. S5A). Closer analysis of co-localization times 268 revealed three types of interactions (fig. S5B). First, extremely short co-localizations (0.23 ± 0.10 s, 269 24.5 %), which most likely constitute collisions without nucleosome binding. Second, short associa270 tions (16.6 ± 3.2 s, 41.3 %) that were represented by complexes binding dynamically to nucleosomes 271 in-between 1D searches. These interaction times are in line with previously reported nucleosome 272 occupancy times of other remodelers [27]. Third, we also detected a cohort of extremely long-lived 273 co-localizations (1200 ± 660 s, 34.1 %) that were mostly characterized by complexes already bound 274 to a nucleosome at the start of recording and that remained statically bound until they dissociated 275 or photobleached (fig. S5A). 85.3 % of INO80 molecules (29/34), for which we could determine 276 the method of association, associated with the nucleosome through 1D searches, as opposed to 3D 277 searches (fig. S5C).

Contrasting a previous study of RSC and ISWI [27], INO80 did not induce long-range nucleosome translocation, most likely because remodeling distances were below our resolution limit. 280 To ensure that the used INO80 complex was active even in its labeled form, we confirmed the 281 remodeling capability with gel-based sliding assays (fig. S5D).

No indication of H2A.Z/H2B-dimer eviction by INO80

Specific eviction of H2A.Z-histones by INO80 in vivo and in vitro has been a controversial topic in INO80 research, with some studies detecting radical changes in H2A.Z distribution across 285 the genome upon INO80 deletion [47], while others observe no differences [48]. Labeling of the 286 H2B-histone allowed us to observe whether INO80 evicts H2A.Z/H2B-dimers from λ-DNA during 287 our assay. While we could not distinguish whether steps in fluorescent signals were caused by 288 photobleaching or H2A.Z/H2B eviction, we were able to compare the time until fluorophore signal 289 disappearance of nucleosomes bound by INO80, with those of nucleosomes imaged in absence of 290 INO80. Both were well-described by a single exponential distribution with a mean lifetime of 291 193.0 ± 23.1 s and 244.0 ± 28.6 s, respectively (fig. 5D). The disappearance rate between both 292 categories showed no significant difference (p = 0.29, log-rank test), indicating that INO80 did not 293 substantially enhance the eviction rate for H2A.Z/H2B-dimers in our assay.

INO80 does not stably bind to DNA-bound Reb1

Apart from DNA sequence, nucleosome positioning by INO80 is influenced by general regulatory 296 factors (GRFs), which bind DNA at defined motif sites and have been proposed to act as specific 297 barriers to the remodeling process and thereby shape nucleosome positioning [7, 20]. Whether this 298 type of regulation requires INO80 binding to GRFs or whether these proteins just act as passive 299 roadblocks for remodeling is unclear. To answer these questions we purified and labeled the GRF 300 Reb1 from S. cerevisiae and assessed its integrity on DNA curtains. Purified Reb1 was functional 301 and bound to known stong binding sites on λ-DNA (fig. S5E). To test whether INO80 and Reb1 302 interact on DNA, we loaded both proteins in consecutive steps onto DNA curtains (fig. 5E). We 303 did not observe binding of INO80 from solution to DNA-bound Reb1. Additionally, we did not 304 observe any simultaneous binding of both proteins. This was unchanged when we reversed the 305 order of injections or co-incubated INO80 and Reb1 for five minutes prior to injection. We then 306 analyzed interactions between INO80 and Reb1 arising through encounters during INO80 1D search 307 (fig. 5F). Co-localization times were extremely short with only 13 % of associations being longer 308 than 2 s and a median of 0.80 ± 0.19 s, suggesting that INO80 does not specifically interact with 309 DNA-bound Reb1 for extended periods of time (fig. 5C). Hence, we conclude that Reb1 functions 310 as a passive barrier for INO80 during nucleosome positioning.

Nucleosomes and DNA-bound Reb1 act as barriers for INO80 diffusion

Finally, to understand how INO80 locates the promoter and the +1 nucleosome within a crowded chromatin environment, it is crucial to uncover how INO80’s path along DNA during 1D searches 314 is affected by collision or interaction with other proteins. We therefore asked whether nucleosomes 315 or Reb1 might act as roadblocks for INO80, confining the complex within subsegments of the 316 promoter. Accordingly, we divided all interactions of INO80 with nucleosomes and Reb1 that 317 showed 1D diffusion before and after, into block and bypass events (fig. 5G). Blocks were defined 318 as events where INO80 stays on the same side of the interaction partner; all remaining interactions 319 were defined as bypasses. INO80 was clearly confined by both proteins with 90 % of nucleosome 320 (81/90) and 98 % of Reb1 interactions (51/52) resulting in a block. We then explicitly looked at 321 longer co-localizations with nucleosomes (>5 s), as in these cases INO80 interacts unequivocally 322 with nucleosomes and passive collisions are excluded (fig. S5F). These events showed a similar 323 picture with 86 % (25/30) of interactions categorized as blocks. This indicates that nucleosome324 bound INO80 preferably disengages to the same direction had bound from and does not hop over

[Page 12]

the nucleosome. Together, these results suggest that INO80 diffusion is blocked by both collisions and interactions with nucleosomes and Reb1. Additionally, these observations validate our earlier 327 findings that INO80 mostly uses a sliding mechanism to diffuse along DNA.

DISCUSSION DNA binding and promoter targeting

Depending on the measurement method each S. cerevisiae cell nucleus harbors 57 000 to 60 000 nucleosomes [49]. Of these, only 4500 to 5500 (7.5 to 9.6 %) constitute possible +1 nucleosomes 332 [48, 50], the main target of INO80 [14]. A unique trait highlighting +1 nucleosomes is the unilateral 333 presence of long stretches of free neighboring promoter DNA. Our experiments clearly demonstrate 334 that INO80 binds strongly to DNA and prefers furthermore certain DNA shape properties, e.g. 335 high propeller twist (Ψ) and high electrostatic potential (EP) (fig. S2G). Here, INO80 exhibits 336 a notably increased on- and reduced off-rate, resulting in accumulation of the complex on these 337 sequences (fig. 2G). Intriguingly, TATA-less promoter and ORI DNA in S. cerevisiae is greatly

[Page 13]

enriched in poly(dA:dT) sequence tracts that are characterized by especially high Ψ and high EP [28, 51]. Closer inspection poly(dA:dT)-tracts in promoter sequences revealed further that they 340 are positioned next to each bordering nucleosome and that their strength positively correlates 341 with direction of transcription [10, 28] (fig. 6A). Given the strong shape preferences we observed 342 by INO80, these asymmetrically positioned poly(dA:dT) tracts could guide the complex towards 343 promoters in general and towards +1 nucleosomes in particular and explain how differential recog344 nition of +1 and −1 nucleosomes is achieved. In general, INO80 shows a high preference for long 345 linkers in nucleosome binding and remodeling assays [18, 40, 41, 45], which can be explained by 346 its structure. With the N- and A-module the complex possesses two DNA-binding modules, which 347 bind extranucleosomal DNA in tandem with a combined footprint of ≈70 bp [14]. Linkers within 348 gene bodies, on the other hand, are short in S. cerevisiae with a length of only ≈18 bp [52]. Combined, these findings are consistent with a model where steric restrictions prevent INO80 350 from proper binding within the gene body (fig. 6B). In consequence, INO80 is targeted to NDRs 351 through recognition of poly(dA:dT)-tract signals within yeast promoters and ORIs and thereby 352 guided into proximity of flanking nucleosomes. The fact that poly(dA:dT)-tract enrichment is 353 mostly missing in promoters with a TATA-box, might imply a heightened importance for +1354 nucleosome positioning by INO80 at TATA-less promoters. Indeed, TATA-less promoters show 355 more uniform +1 nucleosome positioning and recent studies indicate that in absence of a canonical 356 TATA-box, effective assembly of the pre initiation complex is reliant on the position of the +1 357 nucleosome [53].

1D search at a crowded promoter

Once INO80 is bound at an NDR, it needs to associate with the neighboring +1 nucleosome. Here, we observed that INO80 performs 1D diffusion along DNA (fig. 1A–D, fig. 2A) and pre361 sented examples of dynamic interactions with H2A.Z-containing nucleosomes (fig. 5B). It is well362 established that nuclear proteins, which act on DNA or DNA-bound targets, combine diffusion in 363 3D space and 1D along DNA to reduce search times for their targets in a process called ‘facilitated 364 diffusion’ [23, 33, 54–56]. The majority of interactions we recorded were initiated directly through 365 1D searches (fig. S5C), showing that INO80 interactions with +1 nucleosomes through prior 1D 366 searches represent a possible pathway of association in vivo. Our observations therefore reinforce 367 the notion that chromatin remodeling complexes use facilitated diffusion, which is supported by 368 other recent single-molecule studies with chromatin remodelers RSC, IWS2 and SWR1 [26, 27]. 369 Note that we cannot exclude that the ratio of 1D to 3D association might be biased towards 1D 370 association, due to the scarcity of nucleosomes on our DNA templates. By closer analysis of 1D diffusion kinetics, we found that INO80 diffuses mostly by sliding along 372 the helical pitch of the DNA (fig. 2A,D). In line with this, nucleosomes and the GRF Reb1 acted 373 as roadblocks for INO80 movement (fig. 5B,F,G). Additionally, we observed that INO80 continued 374 1D searches after interacting with nucleosomes and preferably dissociated back into the same 375 direction it bound from (fig. S5F). This preference can be explained by the tandem placement 376 of its DNA-binding modules on the entry site of the extranucleosomal DNA. Dissociation into 377 the opposite direction would require big conformational changes, i.e. un- and rebinding of both 378 modules onto the DNA on the exit side of the nucleosome. Not being able to bypass nucleosomes, 379 even by dissociation after remodeling, could capture INO80 within the NDR and prevent “spilling” 380 into intragenic regions (fig. 6C). Coupled with poly(dA:dT) tract-mediated DNA binding, this 381 mechanism might drive differential +1 nucleosome remodeling by INO80. In addition, it promotes 382 repositioning of nucleosomes into the NDR, by assuring that INO80 binds them in the correct 383 orientation.

Simultaneously the extranucleosomal DNA is probed by the upstream A- and N-module. Strong binding of the A-module to an A-tract or physical collision of the N-module with Reb1 lead to fixation of the DNA. As the motor continues to translocate DNA, positive torsional strain is build up, which ultimately might cause eviction of the complex.

Intersegmental transfers

In areas with high DNA density, we repeatedly observed INO80 and Ct. ∆N-INO80 complexes translocating to a neighboring DNA strand without changes in y-position (fig. S2A–D, fig. S3D), 387 indicating the occurrence of intersegmental transfers. Intersegmental transfers have been shown 388 to increase efficient target site search in theoretical models and in single-molecule experiments for 389 small DBPs by reducing local oversampling, especially in environments with high DNA density, 390 where many transfer opportunities arise [24, 57, 58]. It is tempting to speculate that to achieve 391 this, INO80 might—like ISWI—use a “monkey-bar” mechanism to transfer to the neighboring 392 DNA-strand, a bridging mechanism that would be available to protein complexes with multiple

[Page 15]

DNA-binding domains/subunits that are flexibly connected [59, 60] (fig. 6D). In addition, all INO80 variants were able to jump in cis, most likely through dissociation and reassociation onto the 395 same DNA (fig. S2E, fig. S3G). Enhancement in mobility through intersegmental transfers could 396 increase overall +1 nucleosome targeting, especially in euchromatic regions or in the context of 397 transcriptional condensates, where NDRs are thought to be in close proximity [61].

Implications for nucleosome remodeling

After association with the +1 nucleosome, INO80 pulls it towards the NDR [5]. For accurate positioning, INO80 remodeling has to be stopped through negative regulation by external cues. So 401 far, two such cues have been identified indirectly, through analysis of nucleosome positions in vitro 402 and in vivo. The first cue includes blocking of DNA translocation by a barrier protein, bound to 403 DNA (e.g. Reb1), through an unknown mechanism [7, 10], while the second involves DNA shape 404 features within the nucleosomal and extranucleosomal DNA, which impede the remodeling process 405 [10, 21]. Note, that promoters with Reb1 motif also contain positioned DNA shape features and 406 current data indicates a synergizing effect of both cues. Inhibition through a physical barrier might include specific interactions between the barrier 408 and INO80 through protein-protein-contacts with the N-module, which inhibit INO80’s remodeling 409 capabilities allosterically. Alternatively, a passive barrier bound strongly to DNA could be enough 410 to prevent INO80 from reeling in further DNA and specific contacts would not be required. We 411 showed that Reb1, bound to its motif, is a potent barrier for INO80 diffusion, but strikingly, 412 we did not observe long-lived interactions that imply specific binding (fig. 5E–G). Therefore, our 413 observations are consistent with the passive barrier model. In line with this, Reb1 can act as 414 a regulator for both +1 nucleosome positions at bidirectional promoters in vivo, and both ORC 415 and Reb1 can perform this function irrespective of motif orientation [7, 10]. Furthermore, to our 416 knowledge, no common interaction motifs between INO80 and any of the proposed barrier proteins 417 have been found so far. Inhibition through DNA shape features on the other hand requires tracking and read-out of 419 DNA shape by INO80 during nucleosome remodeling. Ct. ∆N-INO80, which represents the min420 imally required complex for functional and competent nucleosome positioning, displayed potent 421 DNA shape-readout during DNA scanning (fig. 3C,E, fig. S3B,C), demonstrating that DNA shape 422 modulates INO80 binding properties dynamically during the scanning process. Further, we ob423 served that the A-module can individually recognize high Ψ DNA and is most likely responsible 424 for recognition of structural DNA features by INO80 (fig. 3E, fig. S3E,F). In agreement with this, 425 we showed that recognition of a high-Ψ motif within linker DNA by the A-module can lead to 426 abortion of the remodeling process (fig. 4A–D). This observation is consistent with DNA shape 427 profiles of +1 nucleosomes, remodeled by INO80 in vitro, which show high Ψ values at −110 to 428 −125 bp from the nucleosome dyad, where the N-terminal end of the A-module is expected to bind 429 the DNA [10]. Furthermore, poly(dA:dT)-tracts at TATA-less genes in vivo are positioned at the 430 same distance relative to the +1 nucleosome dyad [28]. While the molecular details of DNA shape 431 recognition remain unclear, the set of shape features preferred by INO80 imply a readout of the 432 negative electrostatic potential within the minor groove through arginines and lysines (fig. S2G) 433 [37]. This is supported by the findings of a recent study, which showed that mutation of four 434 positively charged amino acids at the N-terminal end of the A-module lead to a severe speed-up 435 of remodeling and ATP hydrolysis, while drastically reducing the DNA binding capabilities of the 436 A-module [19]. Taken together, these findings suggests that inhibition of remodeling by INO80 through DNA 438 shape is connected to stronger binding by the A-module. Interestingly, this indicates that remod399

[Page 16]

eling inhibition through a barrier or through DNA shape features may act by a similar mechanism, namely by fixing the DNA at a certain position in front of the ATPase domain. Although anchoring 441 of the DNA provides a mechanism to prevent effective DNA translocation, it seems counterproduc442 tive for dissociation of the complex. Intriguingly, current models predict overwinding of the DNA 443 in front of the motor during remodeling [42]. Strong binding of the A-Module might enhance this 444 effect: as the motor goes through additional ATP-cycles, the deformation stress of the DNA cannot 445 be resolved by rotation anymore (fig. 6E). This would cause additional positive torsional strain, 446 which is associated with minor groove narrowing [62] and could therefore stabilize A-module-DNA 447 interactions even further. However, whether these twist deformations could directly cause evic448 tion of the complex by disrupting the binding of the ATPase motor or whether they might induce 449 conformational changes that lead to eviction has to be investigated by future research.

Conclusion

Our results demonstrate that both the target-site search of INO80 and its capacity to accurately position nucleosomes are closely connected to its ability to dynamically read out DNA shape 453 features. Intriguingly, the remodeling activities of others remodelers like ISW1a or RSC are also 454 modulated by these features [63]. In light of these findings, DNA shape patterns, originating 455 through poly(dA:dT)-tracts, emerge as central cues, around which remodeler recruitment, activity 456 and therefore promoter architecture is organized, opposing previous theories of intrinsic nucleosome 457 exclusion mechanisms. Additionally, our observations underpin recent studies, which show that the 458 concept of facilitated diffusion extends to chromatin remodelers. In the case of INO80, 1D and 3D 459 diffusion are combined with characteristics of its molecular architecture to exploit traits that high460 light target nucleosomes and thereby maximize engagement. Taken together, our study supports 461 the notion that remodelers have evolved into complex machines that are able to simultaneously 462 integrate multiple forms of information to construct diverse chromatin landscapes.

DNA curtains

Flow cell preparation

Custom made flow cells were assembled from silica-fused slides (UQG Optics) grafted with chromium barriers produced via E-beam lithography and cover slips with a double-sided tape as 664 described previously [29]. Flow cell loading and lipid master mix preparation was adapted from a previous protocol [29]. 666 In brief, 100 mg DOPC (Avanti 850375P-200mg) dissolved in 1 mL chloroform, 1 mL DOPE-PEG 667 (Avanti 880130C-25mg) and 50 μL DOPE-biotin (Avanti 870273C-25mg) were mixed and stored ◦ 668 at −20 C. 100 μL Master Mix was dried using N2 followed by applying a vacuum for 16 h and 669 subsequently resolved in 2 mL lipid buffer (10 mM Tris pH 7.5, 200 mM NaCl). Lipids were sonicated 670 five times for 1 min with 1 min pauses on ice (amplitude 20 %, duty cycle 20 %), filtered through ◦ 671 0.22 μm PVDF filters and stored at 4 C for 3 to 4 weeks. Lipids were diluted 1:10 in lipid buffer 672 containing 20 mM MgCl2 and flow cells were incubated four times for 5 min with 200 μL diluted 673 lipids. After washout, flow cells were incubated with 2 μL of 1 mg/mL anti-digoxigenin (produced 674 in house) in 500 μL lipid buffer for 15 min, followed by 5 μL streptavidin (Carl Roth) in 1 mL 675 BSA buffer (20 mM HEPES pH 8.0, 1 mg/mL BSA, 4 mM Mg(OAc)2 ). After this, 20 pM naked or 676 chromatinized λ-DNA in BSA buffer was added in four steps with 5 min incubation time.

Microscope setup

DNA curtain experiments were carried out on a prism-type TIRF microscope (Nikon Eclipse 679 Ti2), equipped with three illumination lasers (488, 561 and 640 nm Coherent OBIS), an electron 680 multiplying charged coupled camera (iXon Life, Andor) and a syringe-pump-driven microfluidics 681 system supplying the sample chamber. All experiments were carried out using an OptoSplit II 682 (Cairn Research) with a HC BS 640 beam splitter (AHF Analysetechnik) and additional filters 683 (585/65 BP + 700/75 BP, Thorlabs). Videos were recorded in NIS Elements (Nikon) and analyzed 684 in Igor Pro 8 (Wavemetrics) using custom written code (see below). Videos were aquired with a 685 frame rate and illumination time of 50 ms for all experiments with the exception of Ct. ∆N-INO80, 686 where 200 ms were used.

Single-protein diffusion

For single-protein diffusion assays on λ-DNA, the protein was diluted in 80 μL DNA curtains 689 buffer and immediately injected into the flow cell with a constant flow of 0.3 mL/min. The flow 690 was stopped when the protein concentration reached its peak and interactions were observed for 691 up to 8 min per video. Protein was removed from λ-DNA with a 2 M NaCl wash before another 692 injection. Single-molecule measurements were performed in DNA curtains buffer (20 mM HEPES 693 pH 8.0, 1 mg/mL BSA, 4 mM Mg(OAc)2 , 1 mM ATP, 1 mM DTT, 80 mM KGlu if not indicated

[Page 22]

otherwise). Ct. ∆N-INO80 was measured at 60 mM NaCl and data shown for wt-INO80 alone was measured at 120 mM KGlu. Protein concentrations injected varied between 125 to 250 pM for 696 INO80 variants, 2 nM for dCas9 and 75 pM for Reb1.

Nucleosome bleaching, INO80–Reb1 and INO80–nucleosome interaction

To determine nucleosome bleaching rates, flowcells were prepared using chromatinized λ-DNA (see above). The flowcell was washed with 350 mM NaCl to remove partially formed nucleosomes 700 or labeled histones bound to DNA and remaining nucleosomes were imaged until they had all 701 bleached. For remodeler interaction experiments, INO80 was then flushed in and the flow was 702 stopped before live-imaging was initiated. For Reb1 interaction assays, INO80 and Reb1 were injected either sequentially or incubated 704 for 10 min at a 10x concentration before dilution with DNA curtains buffer and injection into the 705 flowcell. Concentrations used were the same as in single protein diffusion experiments.

Mononucleosome sliding assays

To analyze INO80 sliding activity dependent on linker DNA shape, 50 nM S.cerevisiae INO80 was incubated with 100 nM of 6-FAM-labeled nucleosomes in sliding buffer (25 mM HEPES pH 8.0, ◦ 709 60 mM KCl, 7 % glycerol, 0.1 mg/mL BSA, 0.25 mM DTT) at 25 C. The reaction was started by 710 addition of 1 mM ATP complexed with 2 mM MgCl2 and stopped at different timepoints (1, 2, 5, 711 10, 20 and 60 min) by addition of Lambda DNA (0.15 mg/mL; NEB). An ATP-negative control 712 was taken before the start of the reaction and treated like the last sample of the time series. 713 Nucleosome species were separated by native polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) on a 3 714 to 12 % acrylamide bis-tris gel (Invitrogen) and visualized using a Typhoon imaging system (GE 715 Healthcare). Sliding assays to control for sliding activity of labeled endogenous S.cerevisiae INO80 were 717 conducted with 2 nM INO80 and 100 nM of 6-FAM-labeled nucleosomes. The rest of the assay was 718 performed as described above.

Data analysis

Statistical analysis

Statistical analysis was performed using Igor Pro 8 (Wavemetrics). Respective type of test is 722 stated in the main text, figure caption or methods section. Significance was set at p < 0.05 and 723 indicated using the following nomenclature: p > 0.05 = ns.; p < 0.05= *; p < 0.01= **; p < 0.001= 724 ***; p < 0.0001= ****. The results were reproducible and conducted with established internal 725 controls. All samples that met proper experimental conditions were included in the analysis.

Single molecule tracking

Video files were analyzed using custom-written software in Igor Pro (Wavemetrics). Protein trajectories were localized using an algorithm inspired by DAOSTORM [68] and tracked using 729 an in-house Markov chain Monte Carlo-based algorithm. Trajectories were manually screened for 730 detections of particles that displayed exceedingly small (surface-bound) movement or unexpectedly

[Page 23]

bright or dim fluorescence (typically <10 %) and these trajectories were filtered out. Trajectories with less than 30 detections were also omitted from diffusion and binding analysis. Diffusion analysis

Mean squared displacements (MSDs) MSD(n∆t) = (yt − yt+n∆t )2 ,

n = 1, 2, . . .

were determined from tracking data. The uncertainty of the MSD values were estimated using a published procedure [69] and used as weights for fits to the model M (t) = 2Dt + 2σ 2 ,

where σ is the localization noise. At short time delays, the MSDs are dominated by the relaxation time of the thermally fluctuating DNA, rather than movement of proteins along the DNA. Based 739 on tracking of non-moving dCas9 molecules, the threshold where the DNA movement dominates 740 was determined and MSD data with t < 200 ms were excluded from the fit. Fits were performed 741 up to n = 100 ≡ 5 s for all proteins except Ct. ∆N-INO80, where n = 10 ≡ 2 s was used. Time742 varying diffusion was identified by the same procedure applied to a sliding window of fixed length 743 that was scanned along the trajectory. Errors for median diffusion coefficients are 68 % intervals 744 from bootstrapping.

The DNA propeller twist Ψ for each basepair was predicted using Deep DNAshape [38, 70] and averaged in 1 kbp bins.

Errors are 68 % intervals 765 from bootstrapping. Binding events were binned according to the first detected localization of a 766 trajectory, while mean lifetimes were binned according to the mean position of a trajectory along 767 DNA. Interaction analysis

Interactions of INO80 with nucleosomes and Reb1 were determined manually from Kalman776 filtered tracks. Filtering was used to suppress the impact of fluctuations that are caused by 777 movement of the DNA. To ensure that candidates for collisions are bound to the same DNA, 778 the transversal movement perpendicular to the end-to-end vector of the DNA for both candidates 779 was analyzed. Positive correlation and an cross-correlation decay compatible with the transversal 780 relaxation time of the DNA was used as an indicator for binding to the same DNA. Interactions 781 with 1D searches before and after the interaction were further separated into block if both 1D 782 searches happened on the same side of the protein, or bypass otherwise. It should be noted that, 783 using this definition, a bypass does not necessitate a jump of INO80 over the protein constituting 784 a barrier, but can include specific binding to the protein followed by and 1D diffusion on the other 785 side.

Eviction assay — Photobleaching

To assess H2A.Z/H2B dimer eviction by INO80, nucleosomes imaged with and without INO80 788 injection were tracked and the time durations until fluorescence signal loss were determined manu789 ally from fluorescence trajectories. Lifetimes of nucleosome fluorophores imaged without injection 790 of INO80 were calculated and nucleosomes surviving beyond the end of the recording were marked 791 as censored. For fluorophores of nucleosomes imaged with INO80, only the intervals during inter792 action with INO80 were considered and nucleosomes without bleaching/eviction events during the 793 interaction were also marked as censored. Collected lifetimes were then compared using log-rank 794 tests and survival plots were created using the Kaplan-Meier method [71]. Error bars are 68 % 795 intervals from bootstrapping.

Sliding assay analysis

Band intensities were analyzed using ImageLab 6.1 (Bio-Rad) and IGOR Pro 8. Previous experiments have shown that hexasomes are remodeled by INO80 and that fully remodeled 0H80 799 hexasomes co-migrate with unremodeled 0N80 nucleosomes in native acrylamide bis-tris gels [12]. 800 Nucleosome purification can contain trace amounts of hexasomes, visible as slight bands below the

[Page 25]

0N80 nucleosome. To prevent erroneous calculation of the unremodeled nucleosome band, it was corrected for the amount of remodeled hexasome present at each timepoint. For that, the fraction 803 of all nucleosome species including the hexasome were determined at every timepoint. Then, to 804 determine how much hexasome was remodeled, the fraction of the hexasome band was compared 805 to the first measured timepoint (1 min) and the difference was subtracted from the unremodeled 806 nucleosome band (U ). Finally, to determine whether nucleosomes with a poly(dA:dT)-tract at 807 SHL −12/13 were remodeled more slowly, all nucleosome bands (unremodeled, intermediates, fully 808 remodeled) were normalized to 100 % and fully remodeled (R) band fractions for different nucle809 osomes at each timepoint were compared using a two-tailed paired t-test. The paired replicates 810 were performed in succession using the same solutions and vial of INO80 complex. The order of 811 conditions was switched for each pair.
